title: Costs: Steep public liability bills mean 'fun stuff is going to get cancelled'
full_text:
Soaring public liability insurance bills are hitting businesses and not-for-profits, with warnings that adventure tourism, music festivals and volunteer activities such as scouts could all be off the cards if Australia follows the US and becomes an increasingly litigious society.
Neville Tomkins, who chairs the internal governance committee of Scouts Australia, said that one state branch had seen a 168 per cent increase in public liability insurance last year, with an average increase of 35 per cent across branches nationwide. "As a not-for-profit community-based organisation, we cannot sustain that," he said.
Policies are increasingly excluding some activities such as downhill mountain bike riding or charging excesses in the hundreds of thousands, making it harder to offer activities such as flying foxes and rock climbing, he said.
Business NSW chief executive Daniel Hunter said insurance costs were also making some companies more risk-averse because they were finding it difficult to get coverage, stifling innovation.
"My fear for Australia is we are going down this very litigious and insurance-based society ... down that [US] path. The cost and complexity of claims, and we're a claims-friendly jurisdiction, is just going up every year," he said.
Hunter said New Zealand, with its capped compensation for each form of personal injury, had managed to keep public liability insurance costs low.
"Half of Australia is going to Queenstown for their adventure tourism because we can't do it here, you can't do the rafting, you can't do the bungee jumping, and it just seems ridiculous," he told The Australian Financial Review Insurance Summit on Tuesday. "We're going down a path where all the fun stuff is going to get cancelled."
The Emergency Services Levy, currently under review by the NSW government, came in for criticism both by insurers and the business sector for adding to insurance costs. Hunter said the levy was adding 23 per cent to business insurance premiums in NSW.
While Business NSW wants the Minns government to fund scrapping the levy from general revenue, Hunter acknowledged the budget may not be able to afford it. "I think the models they're considering at the moment really do probably lighten the load a bit more on residential insurance policyholders and make the load actually heavier for business," he said, calling for businesses to be no worse off.
Panelists also discussed the legacy of workers' compensation reforms in NSW and other states, after an emergency levy report arguing they could simply push injured workers onto other forms of social safety net, including total and permanent disability insurance through super.
Hunter said reform was necessary because the "frightening" do-nothing scenario was 20 per cent premium increases year-on-year.
Daniel Walton, from claims manager EML, said that workers' compensation premiums had in part been driven by the way courts had broadened the scope, resulting in claims for "things that weren't necessarily always thought of as a psychological injury, an interpersonal dispute between colleagues, or being overlooked for a promotion".
Christine Cupitt, chief executive of the Council for Australian Life Insurers, said life insurance is seeing a "really rapid increase in the rate" of mental health claims.
"The rate of claim for people in their 30s is seven times 732 per cent more than it was 10 years ago," she said.
Cupitt said insurance was "not built with mental ill-health in mind".
"We don't want a situation where anybody is forced into labelling themselves totally and permanently disabled in order to access financial support and get back on their feet."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Karp
heading: Life Insurance
